_meta.title: Pipeline Attrition Estimates by Drug Modality
_meta.source: BIO Industry Analysis 2021 + Nature Reviews Drug Discovery
_meta.note: IMPORTANT: Phase counts are industry-wide estimates, NOT exact trial counts. Success rates are derived from pooled analyses of drug development programs across multiple years. Actual numbers vary by company, indication, and year. These represent typical industry dynamics, not a specific snapshot. Use for illustrative/educational purposes; do not cite as precise statistics.
_meta.how_to_refresh: Update from BIO annual Clinical Development Success Rates report (bio.org) and Nature Reviews Drug Discovery systematic reviews of phase transition rates. FDA CDER's 'Novel Drug Approvals' pages can be cross-referenced for approved count accuracy.

data.notes: Largest pipeline category. Low success rate partly due to broad scope. Oncology small molecules have higher attrition than CNS or metabolic.
Higher success rate than small molecules due to target specificity and validated biology. Phase II→III transition higher than other modalities.
Narrow therapeutic window causes high Phase I toxicity attrition. New-generation ADCs (high DAR, bystander effect) improving outlook.
Unusual attrition profile: many Phase I trials (proof of concept), but regulatory, manufacturing, and efficacy challenges reduce Phase III count. Based on ASGCT Q4 2024 pipeline report.
data.key_source: ASGCT Gene Therapy Pipeline Report Q4 2024

data.notes: High remission rates but manufacturing complexity and CRS toxicity limit broader success. Allogeneic approaches may improve economics.
GalNAc-siRNA platform has excellent Phase 2→3 success for liver targets. ASOs have variable success by indication and delivery route.
data.key_source: Alnylam R&D presentations 2024 + published RNAi trial analyses

data.notes: High success rate for approved modality. CRS and neurotoxicity are key Phase 1 discontinuation drivers.
Highest estimated success rate, driven largely by GLP-1 peptide class with robust Phase 2 → 3 → approval transitions. Other peptide classes have more typical attrition.
data.key_source: Industry peptide drug development analyses + GLP-1 clinical program data
